title: Dependency Injection
rules: keep container ownership at composition root
do not hide control flow inside registration side effects
avoid service locator patterns in feature code
procedure: Resolve only from composition root or controlled boundary.

primary_when: the request is specifically about dependency injection, container setup, or service registration
the request is about composition through object graphs and explicit lifetimes
must_not_own_when: the request is mainly about runtime patching, assembly reading, or UI layout

solves: how to compose runtime object graphs explicitly
how to register abstractions and implementations with clear lifetimes
how to keep container ownership at the composition root
does_not_solve: feature-local object creation where composition is trivial

core_principles: The composition root owns the container.
Lifetimes should be explicit and justified.
Registration should not hide control flow or startup behavior.
constraints: Feature code must not depend on service locator behavior.
Registration side effects must not become a hidden lifecycle system.
The chosen lifetime model must match the host lifecycle.
decision_rules: Use DI as the owner when object graph composition and service lifetime policy dominate the task.
Prefer straightforward construction for small local object graphs that do not cross boundaries.
Do not introduce container indirection unless it clarifies composition or lifecycle ownership.

preferred_patterns: lifetime policy aligned to host lifecycle
contracts registered before consumers resolve them
avoid_patterns: service locator access in feature code
registration methods with hidden side effects
container ownership spread across unrelated modules

recipes.name: Register a clear runtime object graph
recipes.when: Use when several collaborating systems must be composed with explicit lifetimes.
steps: Resolve only from composition root or a tightly controlled boundary.

pitfalls: Treating the container as a global runtime registry.
Using DI where direct construction would be clearer.
Allowing lifetime mismatches to accumulate quietly.
review_checklist: Is container ownership restricted to the composition root?
Is service locator behavior absent from feature code?
Do registrations avoid hidden startup effects?

references.title: Local Reference: Framework DI
references.interpretation: Use this first for explicit registration order, lifetime policy, and composition-root ownership.
references.why: Use as the primary local guide for DI and service graph composition.

references.title: Local Reference: Framework Contracts
references.interpretation: Use this to ensure registrations stay aligned with narrow contracts rather than concrete leakage.
references.why: Use to align service registration with capability-oriented boundaries.
